cases.answer: Proof-checking is decidable: given p and a target, an algorithm always halts with yes/no on whether p codes a valid T-proof of it (it's primitive recursive). Theoremhood is different: 'is there some proof?' is recursively enumerable but not decidable — you can search for a proof and halt 'yes' if one exists, but if none exists the search need not terminate, so there's no always-halting decider. 'Undecidable' here means no decision procedure exists in principle — it is NOT a claim that an algorithm is merely slow or would eventually answer given more time.

Checking a given proof is decidable (always halts). Theoremhood (does any proof exist?) is r.e. but undecidable: confirmable when true by finding the proof, but not always refutable when false. Undecidable means no always-halting algorithm exists, not that the algorithm is slow.

Verifying a candidate proof is a decidable, mechanical check. But whether a sentence is a theorem is only semi-decidable (r.e.) — you can enumerate proofs and stop if you find one, yet there's no procedure guaranteed to halt when it isn't a theorem. So theoremhood is undecidable in principle, which is not about running time.

Theoremhood is undecidable because the algorithm is just too slow — it would take longer than the age of the universe, but in principle a fast enough computer would eventually decide every sentence. Decidable vs undecidable is really about efficiency.
Both proof-checking and theoremhood are undecidable — there's no way to tell if something is a proof or a theorem, that's what Gödel showed.
Computability theory says some things can be computed and some can't. Gödel and Turing showed limits. Theoremhood is one of the hard ones.
Undecidable means the question is a matter of opinion with no objective answer, like undecidable moral questions; theoremhood is decidable if mathematicians agree on it.
